summary: O'Neil maps her emotional response to Trump's election onto the Kübler-Ross grief model, moving from denial through West Wing binge-watching to anger on New York streets, past bargaining over recounts, through depression, and finally to acceptance. She distinguishes her version of 'depression' as clear-eyed realism, noting she's been in 'war mode' since Occupy Wall Street and never believed Hillary was a strong candidate. The piece culminates in a call to action, emphasizing that acceptance doesn't mean normalization but rather embracing activist roles to protect vulnerable communities in what she calls 'post-fact America.'
key_points: The stages of grief framework captures the collective liberal response to Trump's victory, from cognitive dissonance to grudging acceptance
Bargaining through recounts and electoral college hopes is futile and 'sad to witness'
O'Neil's preparedness stems from her Occupy involvement and recognition that economic grievances from 2009 were never properly addressed
Acceptance means active resistance and community-building, not passive normalization
The distinction between depression and realism reflects different levels of prior political cynicism
money_quotes: We'd wake up in the morning wondering why they were referring to inane tweets on NPR, suddenly realize at lunch time that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau will probably never do anything significant again.
It was dumb rage, as anger always is.
The problem with calling it depression is that people who are realistic, rather than overly optimistic, seem depressed.
I never thought Hillary would win, that she was a good candidate, or that people's resentment and anger had been properly addressed.
I've basically been here, poised for this moment, since Obama introduced HAMP as a shitty and insufficient way to address the financial crisis back in 2009.
So you can call it depression, I just call it reality.
By acceptance I do not mean 'normalization.'
Acceptance is not passive, not in this case. Acceptance means accepting our roles as activists and getting shit done.
key_insight: Political grief must transform into organized resistance rather than passive acceptance or futile bargaining over institutional remedies that won't materialize.
